county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Chambers, Macon, Russell, and Tallapoosa counties. County seat Opelika. Auburn University (1856) is a major research resource. Railroad junction at Opelika. Mix of plantation and piedmont farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Opelika. Created in 1866 from Chambers, Macon, Russell, and Tallapoosa — check parent counties for earlier records. Auburn University (founded 1856) has significant manuscript and local history collections in the Ralph Brown Draughon Library Special Collections. Opelika was an important railroad junction. The county had a mix of plantation agriculture and piedmont farming. Lee County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriage records from 1866. For African American research, check enslaved persons records in the four parent counties and Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Lee County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Athens. One of Alabama's oldest towns. Athens Female Academy (1822). Athens State University (oldest institution in Alabama). Excellent county archives.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Athens. Limestone County records are intact from 1818 — one of Alabama's original counties with good record survival. Athens is one of Alabama's oldest towns, incorporated in 1818. The county was settled early by families from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Carolinas via the Great Valley Road. Athens Female Academy (1822) and Athens State University (oldest in Alabama) have historical records. The Limestone County Archives (county-run) is unusually comprehensive for an Alabama county. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm coverage. The Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society covers this area. Cotton plantation records and slaveholder tax records at ADAH document the antebellum period. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Limestone County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1830 from Butler, Dallas, and Montgomery counties. County seat Hayneville. One of the wealthiest Black Belt counties. Enslaved population far outnumbered whites. Central to 1960s voting rights movement (Lowndes County Freedom Organization).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Hayneville. Lowndes County records are largely intact from 1830. The county was named for a South Carolina congressman and was one of the wealthiest Black Belt counties. The enslaved population far outnumbered the white population before the Civil War. Lowndes County was central to the voting rights movement — the Lowndes County Freedom Organization (1965-1966) was one of the first African American political parties in the Deep South. The Viola Liuzzo murder on the Selma-to-Montgomery highway occurred in Lowndes County. Estate inventories, slaveholder tax records, and Freedmen's Bureau records at ADAH and NARA are essential for African American research. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Lowndes County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Tuskegee. Tuskegee University (Booker T. Washington, 1881) and Tuskegee Airmen (Moton Field). Creek War and removal history. Tuskegee Syphilis Study records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Tuskegee. Macon County records are intact from 1832 (formed from Creek Cession). Tuskegee University (founded 1881 by Booker T. Washington) is critical for African American research — the Tuskegee University Archives have extensive records including the Tuskegee Syphilis Study records and the Tuskegee Airmen archives. Moton Field (Tuskegee Airmen training site) is a National Historic Site. The county was part of the Creek Cession and saw significant Creek resistance before removal. Macon County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records documenting enslaved persons. Freedmen's Bureau records for Macon County are at NARA.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1808 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession — one of the earliest Alabama counties. County seat Huntsville. Site of the 1819 Alabama Constitutional Convention. Redstone Arsenal and NASA Marshall Space Flight Center. Excellent Heritage Room at Huntsville-Madison County Public Library.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Huntsville. Madison County records are intact from 1808 — one of the earliest Alabama counties with excellent record survival. Huntsville was Alabama's first major city and hosted the 1819 constitutional convention. The Huntsville-Madison County Public Library's Heritage Room has extensive genealogical collections including family files, Bible records, cemetery records, and manuscripts. The Madison County Records Center preserves older county records. Redstone Arsenal (1941) and NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center brought thousands of families — check NPRC for military records. The Historic Huntsville Foundation has architectural and family history resources. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. The Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society is headquartered in Huntsville. Federal land patents at BLM GLO document the earliest settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Madison County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Choctaw Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Linden. Named for Napoleon's Battle of Marengo. French Bonapartist settlers founded Demopolis (Vine and Olive Colony, 1817). Black Belt cotton plantation county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Linden. Marengo County records are intact from 1818. Named after Napoleon's Battle of Marengo, the county was settled by French Bonapartist exiles who founded Demopolis in 1817 (the 'Vine and Olive Colony'). This French heritage creates unique records — check French-language documents at ADAH and the Marengo County Historical Society. The county became a major Black Belt cotton producer with large enslaved populations. Bluff Hall in Demopolis has planter family archives. The Gaineswood plantation house has related records. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate, and marriages. For French settler research, check NARA for the Vine and Olive Colony land grant records. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Marengo County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Hamilton. 1883 courthouse fire destroyed many records. Strong Union sentiment during Civil War. Natural Bridge of Alabama is a county landmark.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Hamilton. The 1883 courthouse fire destroyed many records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving copies. Marion County was formed in 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession lands. The county was a strong Unionist area during the Civil War — some residents served in Union Alabama regiments. Natural Bridge, Alabama's longest natural bridge, is in Marion County. The county's economy was based on subsistence farming, timber, and coal mining. The Marion County Heritage Center in Hamilton has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. For early settlers, check BLM GLO federal land patents.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1836 from Cherokee Cession. County seat Guntersville. 1868 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Tennessee River and Lake Guntersville (TVA). Guntersville was an important Cherokee town before removal.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Guntersville. The 1868 courthouse fire destroyed early records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH for microfilm copies and state-level duplicates. Marshall County was formed from Cherokee Cession in 1836. Guntersville sits on the Tennessee River and was an important Cherokee town before removal. Lake Guntersville was created by TVA dam construction in the 1930s. The Marshall County Historical Society has local research materials. The county had both Confederate and Union sympathizers during the Civil War. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. For Cherokee ancestry, check the 1835 Henderson Roll and removal records at NARA.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1812 from West Florida Cession. County seat Mobile. Alabama's only major port city. French (1702), British (1763), Spanish (1780) colonial records. Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception sacramental records from 1704. Oldest Mardi Gras celebration in the US.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Mobile. Mobile County records are intact and among the most extensive in Alabama, dating from 1812 with some records from French (1702-1763), British (1763-1780), and Spanish (1780-1813) colonial periods. Mobile was Alabama's major port city and the center of Gulf Coast trade. The Mobile Municipal Archives and the History Museum of Mobile hold colonial and city records. The Sacramental records of the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Catholic) date from 1704 — among the oldest continuous church records in the Gulf South. The Mobile Public Library Local History and Genealogy Division has extensive research collections. For immigration research, Mobile was a major port of entry — check ship passenger lists at NARA. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. Mobile Mardi Gras records (oldest in the US) may document social connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Mobile County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1812; deed books substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Probate Records -- Probate Judge / ADAH
source_profiles.repository: Mobile County Probate Judge, Mobile / Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, inventories, and administrations for Mobile County; Probate Judge handles wills and estates in Alabama; ADAH has microfilm of historical probate records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Substantially complete from 1812

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Marriage Records -- Probate Judge / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Mobile County Probate Judge, Mobile / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and bonds for Mobile County from 1812; Probate Judge issues marriage licenses in Alabama; FamilySearch has indexed many Alabama marriages

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Mobile County; Alabama first enumerated in 1820 federal census; slave schedules in 1850 and 1860; agricultural and manufacturing schedules available

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Naturalization and Immigration Records
source_profiles.repository: Mobile County Circuit Court / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization petitions and declarations of intention filed in Mobile County courts; industrial workers from Italy, Greece, Eastern Europe, and other countries sought citizenship; circuit court records at county courthouse; NARA holds federal court naturalizations

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Federal Land Patents -- BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Mobile County from the General Land Office; Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government; patents searchable and downloadable at BLM GLO website

source_profiles.source_name: Mobile County Church and Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: ADAH / FamilySearch / Local churches and cemeteries
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian) for Mobile County; cemetery transcriptions at Find A Grave and USGenWeb; ADAH has some microfilmed church records; essential for vital events before state registration (1908)

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1815 from Creek Cession. County seat Monroeville. Harper Lee and Truman Capote hometown. Creek War history (Fort Mims area). One of Alabama's earliest counties with good record survival.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Monroeville. Monroe County records are intact from 1815, making it one of Alabama's earliest counties with good survival. Monroeville is the hometown of Harper Lee (To Kill a Mockingbird) and Truman Capote. The Old Monroe County Courthouse (1903) is a literary landmark. The county was part of the Creek Cession and had significant Creek interactions before and during the Creek War (1813-1814). Monroe County Heritage Museum has local materials. The county's economy was based on cotton, timber, and cattle. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate records, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records. Federal land patents at BLM GLO document original settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Monroe County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants
